meta.title: Cephalometric Diagnosis and Treatment Planning Reference
meta.description: Mapping of cephalometric measurements to clinical diagnoses and treatment plans based on current orthodontic and surgical protocols. References to scientific literature are included where applicable.
sources: Proffit, W.R., Fields, H.W., & Sarver, D.M. (2018). Contemporary Orthodontics, 6th ed.
McNamara, J.A. (1984). A method of cephalometric evaluation. Am J Orthod, 86(6):449-69.
Steiner, C.C. (1953). Cephalometrics for you and me. Am J Orthod, 39(10):729-55.
Ricketts, R.M. (1969). A foundation for cephalometric communication. Am J Orthod, 55(3):219-42.
Jarabak, J.R. (1972). Technique and treatment with light-wire edgewise appliances. Mosby.
Björk, A. (1960). Relationship of the jaws to the cranium. In: Lundström, A. (ed.) Introduction to Orthodontics.
Downs, W.B. (1948). Variations in facial relationships. Angle Orthod, 18(1):44-53.
Burstone, C.J. (1967). Lip posture and its significance in treatment planning. Am J Orthod, 53(4):262-84.
American Board of Orthodontics (ABO) Phase III clinical examination guidelines.
Kim, Y.H. (1974). Overbite depth indicator. Am J Orthod, 65(6):650-68.
Viazis, A.D. (1991). The Viazis cephalometric analysis. J Clin Orthod, 25(8):487-92.
Lagravere, M.O. et al. (2005). Reliability of traditional cephalometric landmarks. Am J Orthod Dentofacial Orthop, 128(5):612-8.
meta.disclaimer: These automated recommendations are for clinical decision support only. Final treatment must be individualized by a qualified orthodontist or maxillofacial surgeon.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.diagnosis: Excessive forward maxillary position relative to cranial base; often associated with Class II malocclusion, convex profile, and nasal step-off.

growing_patient.summary: Growth modification with reverse-pull headgear or facemask (protraction therapy) + maxillary expansion if transverse deficiency exists.
appliances: Rapid maxillary expander (RME) followed by protraction
growing_patient.timeline: Begin at early mixed dentition (age 7–9). Active protraction for 12–18 months.
growing_patient.scientific_reference: Kim et al. (2017) Angle Orthod 87(3):370-6 – Facemask + RME effective for skeletal Class III correction.

adult_non_growing.summary: Orthognathic surgery (LeFort I osteotomy with maxillary impaction/advancement) with preoperative orthodontic decompensation.
procedures: LeFort I maxillary impaction and/or advancement (3–7 mm)
Possible mandibular setback or genioplasty for facial balance
adult_non_growing.pre_surgical_orthodontics: Remove dental compensations (upper incisor retroclination, lower incisor proclination).
adult_non_growing.scientific_reference: Proffit et al. (2011) J Oral Maxillofac Surg 69(11):2833-42 – Surgical‑orthodontic treatment outcomes.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.soft_tissue_implications: Prominent upper lip, increased nasolabial angle (if maxilla advanced), or decreased nasolabial angle (if maxilla anteriorly positioned).

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.diagnosis: Hypoplastic maxilla resulting in midface deficiency; often accompanied by relative mandibular prognathism and Class III malocclusion.

growing_patient.summary: Maxillary protraction (facemask therapy) with or without RME to stimulate anterior maxillary growth.
appliances: Bone‑anchored maxillary protraction (BAMP) for older children
Alternating rapid maxillary expansions and constrictions (Alt‑RAMEC) before protraction
growing_patient.timeline: Begin as early as age 5–7, continue until overjet positive and Class I molar achieved.
growing_patient.scientific_reference: Vaughn et al. (2005) Angle Orthod 75(4):525-32 – Long‑term stability of facemask therapy.

adult_non_growing.summary: Maxillary advancement via LeFort I osteotomy (anterior or total maxillary advancement).
procedures: Consider bone grafting if large advancement
Associated mandibular setback or counterclockwise rotation if needed
adult_non_growing.pre_surgical_orthodontics: Align and decompensate (procline upper incisors, retrocline lower incisors).
adult_non_growing.scientific_reference: Bailey et al. (2001) J Oral Maxillofac Surg 59(7):729-38 – Stability of maxillary advancement.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.soft_tissue_implications: Concave profile, increased nasal projection, shallow nasolabial angle, often improved after maxillary advancement.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.diagnosis: Mandibular excess (prognathism) leading to Class III malocclusion, concave profile, and anterior crossbite.

growing_patient.summary: Growth modulation with chin cup or facemask with maxillary protraction (depending on maxillary position).
appliances: Maxillary expansion + facemask is more effective for skeletal Class III
growing_patient.timeline: Start at early mixed dentition; monitor mandibular growth suppression.
growing_patient.scientific_reference: Sugawara et al. (1990) Am J Orthod Dentofacial Orthop 98(3):231-42 – Chin cup effects.

adult_non_growing.summary: Bilateral sagittal split osteotomy (BSSO) for mandibular setback, often combined with maxillary advancement if indicated.
procedures: LeFort I advancement if maxillary retrusion also present
adult_non_growing.pre_surgical_orthodontics: Decompensate: retrocline lower incisors, procline upper incisors.
adult_non_growing.scientific_reference: Keller et al. (2019) J Oral Maxillofac Surg 77(12):2494-2505 – Stability of BSSO setback.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.soft_tissue_implications: Prominent chin, concave facial profile, deep mentolabial sulcus, improved after mandibular setback.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.diagnosis: Mandibular deficiency, often causing Class II malocclusion, convex profile, and deep bite or open bite.

growing_patient.summary: Functional appliance therapy to enhance mandibular growth and reposition the mandible forward.
growing_patient.timeline: Start during mixed dentition (age 9–11); wear for 12–18 months full‑time, then retainer.
growing_patient.scientific_reference: O’Brien et al. (2003) Am J Orthod Dentofacial Orthop 124(5):487-96 – Twin Block vs. untreated controls.

adult_non_growing.summary: Mandibular advancement via BSSO; if combined with Class II division 2 deep bite, consider vertical control.
procedures: Consider concurrent LeFort I impaction if open bite
adult_non_growing.pre_surgical_orthodontics: Decompensate: retrocline lower incisors, procline upper incisors.
adult_non_growing.scientific_reference: Schreuder et al. (2007) Int J Oral Maxillofac Surg 36(3):220-6 – Stability of BSSO advancement.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.soft_tissue_implications: Retruded chin, convex profile, increased lower lip strain; mandibular advancement improves facial profile.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.diagnosis: Increased lower anterior face height, steep mandibular plane, often associated with anterior open bite and Class II or Class III malocclusion.

growing_patient.summary: Control vertical growth using high‑pull headgear, vertical chin cup, and bite blocks.
appliances: Posterior bite blocks to intrude molars
growing_patient.mechanics: Avoid extrusion of posterior teeth; use TADs for molar intrusion if needed.
growing_patient.scientific_reference: Schudy (1968) Am J Orthod 54(4):233-57 – Vertical dimension control.

adult_non_growing.summary: Surgical impaction of maxilla (LeFort I) with or without mandibular counterclockwise rotation.
procedures: LeFort I impaction (2–5 mm posteriorly more than anteriorly)
BSSO advancement with autorotation of mandible
adult_non_growing.scientific_reference: Reyneke et al. (2002) Int J Adult Orthodon Orthognath Surg 17(2):95-105 – Diagnosis and treatment of open bite.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.soft_tissue_implications: Long face, lip incompetence, gummy smile (if maxillary vertical excess); surgery often improves lip seal.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.diagnosis: Decreased lower facial height, flat mandibular plane, often associated with deep bite and square facial form.

growing_patient.summary: Extrude posterior teeth to increase vertical dimension; avoid high‑pull headgear.
appliances: Bite turbos (anterior) to allow posterior eruption
growing_patient.mechanics: Encourage molar extrusion; treat deep bite with curve‑of‑Spee leveling.
growing_patient.scientific_reference: Arat et al. (2003) Angle Orthod 73(4):374-80 – Deep bite correction.

adult_non_growing.summary: Extrusion of posterior teeth via orthodontics, or surgical lengthening of maxilla (LeFort I downgraft) if severe.
procedures: LeFort I downgraft with bone graft
adult_non_growing.pre_surgical_orthodontics: Level curve of Spee, intrude incisors, extrude molars.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.soft_tissue_implications: Short lower face, deep labiomental fold, often improved with increased vertical dimension.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.diagnosis: Proclined upper and lower incisors with protrusive lips, often seen in certain ethnic groups; may cause lip incompetence and convex profile.

non_extraction.indications: Mild protrusion (2–4 mm) with adequate arch length.
non_extraction.therapy: Distalization of molars, interproximal reduction, proclination acceptance.

extraction.indications: Severe protrusion (> 6 mm), lip incompetence, moderate crowding.
teeth_extracted: Second premolars if anterior open bite
extraction.mechanics: Maximum retraction of incisors with reinforced anchorage (TADs, headgear, Nance arch).

surgical.indications: Severe skeletal protrusion (bimaxillary) with normal jaw relationships but extreme dental protrusion.

diagnosis_treatment_matrix.scientific_reference: Bowman (2008) Am J Orthod Dentofacial Orthop 134(3):420-31 – Extraction vs. nonextraction in bimax protrusion.

cephalometric_measurement_to_diagnosis_m.certainty: High when combined with increased ANB and protrusive upper lip.
Often with negative ANB and Class III malocclusion.
Often with increased ANB, Class II division 1.

cephalometric_measurement_to_diagnosis_m.diagnosis: Class II skeletal pattern (maxillary prognathism or mandibular retrognathism)
Class III skeletal pattern (maxillary retrognathism or mandibular prognathism)

growth_modification.name: Growth Modification (Functional/Orthopedic)
growth_modification.indications: Skeletal Class II or Class III in growing patients (pre‑pubertal or pubertal).
growth_modification.duration: 12–24 months, usually during mixed dentition or early permanent dentition.
growth_modification.evidence_level: High for Class II correction (functional appliances), moderate for Class III (facemask).

orthodontic_camouflage.name: Orthodontic Camouflage (Extraction/Non‑extraction)
orthodontic_camouflage.indications: Mild to moderate skeletal discrepancy (Class II or Class III) in non‑growing patients or those declining surgery.
techniques: Temporary anchorage devices (TADs) for skeletal anchorage
orthodontic_camouflage.scientific_reference: Janson et al. (2007) Am J Orthod Dentofacial Orthop 132(2):158-67 – Camouflage vs. surgery for Class II.

orthognathic_surgery.name: Orthognathic Surgery
orthognathic_surgery.indications: Moderate to severe skeletal discrepancies in adults (or post‑growth adolescents).
orthognathic_surgery.stability: Stable for maxillary advancement; less stable for large mandibular setbacks without rigid fixation.
orthognathic_surgery.scientific_reference: Costa et al. (2016) J Craniomaxillofac Surg 44(9):1257-64 – Surgical stability meta‑analysis.
